narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 60.5% chance that you would fail it. This prediction draws from patterns in data about thousands of past students, including their academic records, test scores, family backgrounds, and more, to help the school make informed admission decisions. For you, as a Black male with an upper-middle-class family background, planning to attend full-time, the model zeroed in on a few key aspects of your profile that shaped this outcome.

The most influential factor pulling the prediction toward passing was your LSAT score of 46, which stands out well above the typical applicant's average of around 37 and strongly signaled your potential to succeed on high-stakes exams like the bar. However, this positive signal was outweighed by your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at 3—meaning you placed in the lower third of your classmates those years, compared to averages around the middle of the pack (5.7 and 5.5). These lower rankings had the strongest negative impacts, pushing the model firmly toward predicting bar failure because they suggest challenges in consistently performing at the top levels needed for law school rigor.

Your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, above the average of 3.21, helped a bit but wasn't among the top drivers here. Overall, your profile compares to typical students as solid in some standardized metrics like LSAT and GPA, but the early academic deciles raised red flags that dominated the decision, even with your full-time commitment and stable background. We know this is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black students like yourself face in competitive academic environments.

To move forward, consider retaking courses or seeking targeted tutoring to boost your class rankings if possible, or gaining practical experience through internships that highlight your strengths. You might also explore programs with bar passage support tailored for diverse students, or reapply after strengthening those decile areas—many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort. Your LSAT shows real promise, so building on that could shift the odds significantly next time.
